examples.title: Simple Client-Server Communication
examples.description: Basic pseudocode demonstrating client-server communication using sockets
examples.code:
// SERVER CODE
BEGIN
 CREATE socket serverSocket
 BIND serverSocket to port 8080
 LISTEN for connections
 
 WHILE true DO
 clientSocket = ACCEPT connection
 REQUEST = RECEIVE from clientSocket
 RESPONSE = PROCESS REQUEST
 SEND RESPONSE to clientSocket
 CLOSE clientSocket
 END WHILE
END

// CLIENT CODE
BEGIN
 CREATE socket clientSocket
 CONNECT clientSocket to server:8080
 REQUEST = "Get Data"
 SEND REQUEST to server
 RESPONSE = RECEIVE from server
 DISPLAY RESPONSE
 CLOSE clientSocket
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the basic client-server model where server waits for connections, processes requests, and returns responses. The socket abstraction hides network details.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) per request where n is request size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of concurrent connections

examples.title: Heartbeat-based Failure Detection
examples.description: Algorithm used to detect node failures in distributed systems using periodic heartbeat messages
examples.code:
// NODE MONITOR PROCESS
BEGIN
 SET nodes = [Node1, Node2, Node3, Node4, Node5]
 SET heartbeatTimeout = 5 seconds
 SET heartbeatInterval = 1 second
 SET nodeStatus = {} // Map of node to status
 
 FOR EACH node IN nodes DO
 nodeStatus[node] = "alive"
 END FOR
 
 // Main monitoring loop
 WHILE true DO
 FOR EACH node IN nodes DO
 IF node.lastHeartbeat + heartbeatTimeout < currentTime THEN
 nodeStatus[node] = "failed"
 TRIGGER failoverProcedure(node)
 LOG "Node " + node + " marked as failed"
 END IF
 END FOR
 WAIT heartbeatInterval
 END WHILE
END

// HEARTBEAT SENDER (on each node)
BEGIN
 WHILE true DO
 SEND heartbeat to monitor
 WAIT heartbeatInterval
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Failure detection is crucial for fault tolerance. This algorithm uses heartbeats - if a node doesn't send a heartbeat within the timeout, it's considered failed and failover procedures are triggered.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of monitored nodes

examples.title: Load Balancer Request Distribution
examples.description: Algorithm for distributing client requests across multiple server instances
examples.code:
// LOAD BALANCER
BEGIN
 SET servers = [Server1, Server2, Server3]
 SET currentIndex = 0
 SET serverWeights = {Server1: 1, Server2: 2, Server3: 1} // weighted round-robin
 
 FUNCTION getNextServer()
 // Round-robin with weights
 count = 0
 FOR i = 1 TO sum(serverWeights) DO
 count = count + 1
 IF count <= serverWeights[servers[currentIndex]] THEN
 RETURN servers[currentIndex]
 END IF
 currentIndex = (currentIndex + 1) % length(servers)
 END FOR
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Handle incoming request
 WHILE true DO
 clientRequest = ACCEPT()
 targetServer = getNextServer()
 FORWARD clientRequest to targetServer
 response = RECEIVE from targetServer
 SEND response to client
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed. This implements weighted round-robin for better resource utilization.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for server selection, O(m) for forwarding where m is message size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(s) where s is number of servers
